turns.content: I'm trying to process a log stream in production and collect all the parsing results into one vector. I want to separate ok cases from errors, but my current approach using flat_map seems wrong because it flattens too much.

You are mixing up iterator combinators. Flat_map transforms each element and flattens the result into a single stream of elements. What you need is to collect a list of Results. You can use partition on an iterator of Option or Result types to separate successes from failures.
Okay, so I have an iterator that yields Result<LogEntry, ParseError>. How do I get a Vec where the first half has Ok entries and the second has Err?

Use partition on your iterator of Results. It splits into two iterators: one for values that are Ok and one for Err. Then collect each into separate vectors using from_iter or by calling collect::<Vec<_>>(). This creates a Vec with the partitioned results.
So I can do let (oks, errs) = log_results.partition(|r| r.is_ok()); and then collect both?
Yes. The partition method on an iterator yields two iterators: one for Ok values and one for Err values. You can call collect::<Vec<_>>() on each to build the final vectors.
Wait, if I just iterate over a Result<_, _> and match it in the closure, won't that work too?
That works but is less idiomatic. Using partition on an iterator of Results is cleaner because it leverages the trait bounds for Iterator and IntoIterator automatically.
Got it, so I should avoid flat_map here since it flattens the structure I need to preserve. And I should use collect with from_iter or explicit collection?
Correct. Use partition followed by collect on each branch. Avoid flat_map when you want to maintain a list of Results rather than flattening them.
One more thing, what if the iterator is lazy and I only need the first 10 results? Should I use take_before or collect with limit?
Use take_n on your iterator before partitioning. You can do log_results.take(10).partition() to limit the stream before splitting it into ok/err vectors.
